items.correct: A unit of DISTANCE - the distance light travels in one year.
items.why: Students hear 'year' and assume time. Light year is distance (about 9.46 trillion km) - it's a useful astronomical scale, not a measure of when something happened.

items.q: Why don't we have a solar eclipse every new moon?
items.correct: The Moon's orbit is tilted, so it usually passes above or below the Sun's apparent position.
items.why: Students think every new moon should eclipse the Sun. The Moon's orbit is tilted about 5 degrees to the ecliptic, so usually the new Moon doesn't align - alignment requires nodes lining up, giving eclipse seasons.

items.q: What does cosmological redshift tell us?
items.correct: Galaxies are receding from us due to the expansion of space.
items.misconception: Galaxies are turning red as they age.
items.random_wrong: Light gets tired and loses energy.
items.why: Students take redshift literally as colour change. It's a WAVELENGTH STRETCH - light waves get longer because space itself expands while light travels, shifting visible light toward the red end of the spectrum.
Correct answer: Gravitational waves. “Radio pulses” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate Schwarzschild radius per solar mass?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 3 km. “30 km” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the approximate Schwarzschild radius (km) of a 5 solar mass black hole?
items.why: Exact answer: 15. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
